meta.title: PRD Reviewer Local/Web UI
meta.summary: A local-first web reviewer UI for PRD.json that gives humans a dashboard, schema-aware section views, validation, traceability, and safe export/writeback while preserving PRD.json as the canonical artifact for AI agents.

stakeholders.name: PRD reviewer
stakeholders.role: Reviews scope, open questions, risks, and decisions

stakeholders.name: Engineering maintainer
stakeholders.role: Owns schema validation, file safety, and implementation

stakeholders.name: Approval owner
stakeholders.role: Signs off on review readiness and release scope

stakeholders.name: Compliance owner
stakeholders.role: Reviews privacy and retention if comments, reviewer identity, or shared deployment are introduced

meta.source_brief: User request on 2026-04-20 to create a PRD for the local/web PRD.json reviewer UI, using the previously recommended shape and preserving agent-friendly PRD JSON.

migrations.migration_path: Add optional top-level project_tracking and preserve 1.0.0 documents as backward-compatible under schema.versions.json.
migrations.notes: Schema 1.1.0 introduces first-class project tracking without forcing migration of existing 1.0.0 PRDs.

migrations.migration_path: Add optional external_refs URI arrays to project-tracking records when external workflow links are available.
migrations.notes: Schema 1.2.0 links durable PRD tracking records to external issues, pull requests, project boards, or sprint iterations without duplicating their live state.

problem.statement: Reviewing PRD.json in this repo is still a document-inspection problem: the artifact is schema-constrained, optimized for machine validation, and difficult for humans to review efficiently without a dedicated interface.
problem.background: This repo already contains schema-defined PRD artifacts, validation tooling, and PRD-focused agent skills. The current viewer PRD captures delivery readiness, risks, questions, and decisions, but it still lacks a first-class project-tracking section for TODOs, issues, blockers, and status notes.
problem.opportunity: A local/web reviewer UI can turn the existing JSON into a navigable, schema-aware review experience with dashboard summaries, section-specific editing, validation, traceability, and safe writeback, while preserving PRD.json as the source of truth.
problem.strategic_fit: The UI complements the existing PRD generation and validation workflow by adding a human review layer without changing the machine-readable JSON contract agents depend on, while also evolving into a reusable framework that a solo developer can apply to later projects with the same viewer, schema discipline, and future MCP access patterns.

evidence.description: README.md describes this repo as a PRD JSON generation skill pack with schema.strict.json used for strict machine validation.
schema.strict.json uses a closed top-level contract and rejects undocumented properties, making schema-aware UI validation and contract evolution important.

viewer/PRD_web_ui.json already tracks readiness, risks, open questions, and decisions separately, but it does not provide one first-class project status surface for pending work, issues found, and blockers.
JSON Forms supports declarative form UIs using JSON Schema plus UI schema, matching the need for schema-driven section editors.
svelte-jsoneditor and vanilla-jsoneditor support JSON tree, table, text, query, transform, and schema validation modes for power-user editing.
React Flow models interactive graphs as nodes and edges, which maps well to requirement, story, decision, dependency, and risk traceability.
VS Code JSON support already provides schema-backed validation, IntelliSense, hovers, formatting, and folding for developer editing workflows.
PostgreSQL recommends jsonb for most application JSON storage, but a DB introduces persistence and migration ownership beyond the MVP.
SQLite includes JSON functions by default in modern versions and can support a local derived index if search or workflow state becomes necessary.

business_goals.statement: Shorten PRD review cycles by letting reviewers assess structure, completeness, validation status, and blockers from one interface.
business_goals.rationale: Reviewers currently have to reconstruct meaning from raw JSON, schema files, and cross-section links.

business_goals.statement: Reduce invalid or incomplete PRD changes before they reach implementation agents.
business_goals.rationale: Agents depend on stable IDs, strict schema compliance, and complete traceability to plan and implement work reliably.

business_goals.statement: Preserve PRD.json as the canonical source of truth while improving human review ergonomics.
business_goals.rationale: The repo is already JSON-first; moving authority to a DB too early would create synchronization and migration risk.

business_goals.statement: Create a foundation for future collaboration features only if review usage proves the need.
business_goals.rationale: Comments, assignments, and approvals may justify a derived DB later, but are not required to validate the core review experience.

business_goals.statement: Turn the viewer into a reusable solo-developer framework for building later projects with the same PRD contract, review UI, and future local MCP support.
business_goals.rationale: If the viewer can refine its own framework while staying local-first and schema-driven, the same stack can be reused to build other projects without restarting the toolchain design from scratch.

user_outcomes.desired_outcome: Can determine whether a PRD draft is ready for approval without reading raw JSON line by line.
user_outcomes.measurement_signal: Median review time decreases and comments focus on substantive scope issues rather than navigation or formatting confusion.

user_outcomes.desired_outcome: Can see missing sections, invalid fields, unresolved questions, and broken links before requesting review.
user_outcomes.measurement_signal: Fewer repair loops are needed after the first review pass.

user_outcomes.desired_outcome: Can validate PRD.json against schema.strict.json and verify safe round-trip save behavior.
user_outcomes.measurement_signal: Every saved PRD passes schema validation and snapshot regression checks.

user_outcomes.desired_outcome: Can consume the same canonical PRD.json plus derived trace summaries without ambiguity or hidden application-only state.
user_outcomes.measurement_signal: Agent task planning references stable IDs and does not require data from a non-canonical database.

success_metrics.measurement_method: Validate every save against schema.strict.json before file write or export.
Regression tests compare loaded, edited, and re-saved PRD.json fixtures for preserved meaning and IDs.

success_metrics.metric: Median time to open and render a PRD
success_metrics.measurement_method: Measure from file open to first fully rendered and validated document state on reference hardware.
Count unique internal users who complete at least one review session in the UI each week during pilot.

success_metrics.unit: percent of loaded PRDs with visible compatibility status
success_metrics.measurement_method: For every loaded PRD, compare meta.schema_contract and schema.versions.json and show exact, backward-compatible, migration-required, or legacy-unversioned status in the UI.

success_metrics.metric: Agent context reduction for repeat PRD work
success_metrics.unit: percent reduction in average agent context bytes for repeated PRD queries once MCP projections are added
success_metrics.measurement_method: Compare full-document agent payload size against derived section, entity, or trace packets served from the future local MCP layer for repeated planning and implementation tasks.

guardrail_metrics.measurement_method: Count rejected saves and confirm no canonical file write occurs.
Track load and validation time across representative small and large PRD fixtures.
Client error telemetry or local structured logs across active reviewer sessions.
Count JSONL rows the reviewer UI cannot parse or cannot map to a savings summary.

guardrail_metrics.unit: percent of PRDs with unsupported required extensions blocked from unsafe editing
guardrail_metrics.measurement_method: Load PRDs with required extension declarations and verify the UI blocks writeback unless the extension is supported or explicitly ignored by an authorized maintainer.

non_goals: The MVP does not replace PRD.json with a separate authoritative database or index.
The MVP does not support concurrent multi-user editing or merge conflict resolution.
The MVP does not auto-generate, rewrite, or invent PRD content without explicit user approval.
The MVP does not publish a public-facing, multi-tenant PRD portal.
The MVP does not require a hosted backend for read-only review, validation, or local export.

assumptions.statement: The reviewer UI is review-first and schema-aware; PRD.json remains the authoritative artifact for v1.
assumptions.validation_plan: Confirm the expected workflow with the repo owner before implementation: read-only review, edit/writeback, or both.

assumptions.statement: schema.strict.json remains the canonical validation contract for the reviewer UI.
assumptions.validation_plan: Wire validation tests directly to schema.strict.json and fail CI if UI validation diverges from the schema.

assumptions.statement: The first useful release can run local-first in a browser or thin local shell without a mandatory backend service.
assumptions.validation_plan: Prototype file open, validation, export, and writeback in the target runtime before building collaboration features.

assumptions.statement: Most reviewers understand PRD concepts but should not need to understand raw JSON structure.
assumptions.validation_plan: Run a pilot review with at least one non-implementation reviewer and collect navigation, terminology, and approval feedback.

assumptions.statement: A database is not justified until there is proven need for concurrent editing, persistent comments, assignments, audit history, or cross-PRD querying.
assumptions.validation_plan: Track pilot requests for collaboration features and revisit the DB decision after at least three complete review cycles.

personas.name: PRD Review Lead
personas.role: Product or operational stakeholder who reviews PRD scope for completeness, clarity, and approval readiness.
goals: Confirm the PRD matches intended project scope.
Catch missing ownership, unresolved questions, and scope drift before approval.
needs: A readable sectioned view that avoids raw JSON scanning.
A blocker summary for open questions, risks, and validation failures.
Fast navigation from summary items to detailed PRD content.
pain_points: Raw JSON is difficult to scan during review sessions.
Important blockers are spread across many sections.
It is hard to tell which items still need signoff.
current_workarounds: Exports PRD content into documents or spreadsheets for review.
Asks a maintainer to summarize what changed.
Uses chat or meetings to track open decisions.
personas.environment: Desktop browser during product or project review sessions.

personas.name: PRD Maintainer
personas.role: Developer or agent maintainer responsible for keeping PRD.json schema-valid, traceable, and safe for downstream agents.
goals: Keep PRD structure valid and publishable.
Catch broken IDs, missing links, and stale fields before review.
needs: Fast schema validation with path-level error reporting.
Coverage checks for linked requirements and stories.
Safe writeback that preserves the last valid canonical file.
pain_points: Broken cross-links are easy to miss in large PRDs.
Repeated edits can introduce duplicate or stale IDs.
current_workarounds: Inspects raw diffs in the editor.
Verifies links by hand before handing the PRD to reviewers.
personas.environment: Local development workstation with CLI tools and browser access.

personas.name: Implementation Trace Agent
personas.role: AI or automation agent that consumes the PRD to plan and implement scoped work with traceability.
goals: Preserve traceability from requirements to implementation work.
Detect missing context before generating tasks or code changes.
needs: Stable IDs and backlinks across requirements, stories, and decisions.
A machine-readable trace summary for the current PRD state.
Visibility into unresolved questions and dependencies.
pain_points: Trace data is scattered across sections.
ID changes can break downstream task mapping.
current_workarounds: Reads the full PRD before acting.
Builds scratch trace maps outside the document.
Asks for clarification when links or dependencies are missing.
personas.environment: Local agent runtime, CLI workspace, and reviewer UI output.

personas.name: Approval Owner
personas.role: Decision-maker who grants final approval once the PRD is internally consistent and review-complete.
goals: Approve only PRDs that are internally consistent.
Keep approval decisions tied to the canonical document.
needs: A clear blocker list before approval is possible.
A visible status for validation, open questions, and must-have coverage.
Confidence that approval does not depend on hidden application state.
pain_points: Approval is slowed by unclear document state.
Open blockers can be missed in long review cycles.
There is no single human-friendly source for review readiness.
current_workarounds: Asks for status in meetings or chat.
Keeps manual approval notes outside the PRD.
Relies on someone else to summarize review outcomes.
personas.environment: Browser-based review workflow and approval meetings.

functional.title: Open canonical PRD in browser
functional.description: The reviewer UI must open a PRD.json file in a browser session and treat that file as the canonical document for review and optional editing.
functional.rationale: A browser front end is useful only if it works directly against the canonical file rather than a duplicate authoritative store.
acceptance_criteria: A reviewer can open the UI in a browser and load PRD.json from a local workspace path or file picker.
The UI shows the active PRD source path or source label for the current session.
The document can be viewed without importing it into a separate authoritative database first.

functional.title: Show review dashboard
functional.description: The UI should present a human-friendly dashboard that summarizes the current PRD's structure, project status, validation state, risks, decisions, and open blockers.
functional.rationale: A summary view reduces time spent reading raw JSON and helps reviewers focus on incomplete or risky areas.
acceptance_criteria: The dashboard shows counts or presence indicators for top-level PRD sections, functional requirements, non-functional requirements, user stories, project-tracking pending work, project-tracking issues, project-tracking blockers, open questions, proposed decisions, high-score risks, validation issues, compatibility status, and trace links.

The dashboard highlights validation errors, active project-tracking blockers, open questions, high-score risks, missing required content, proposed decisions, and broken traceability references as separate review signals.
A reviewer can navigate from each dashboard summary control or blocking signal to the relevant section in one action, and the destination section is visibly selected.

functional.title: Validate against strict schema
functional.description: The reviewer UI must validate the loaded PRD.json against schema.strict.json and surface violations at section and field level.
functional.rationale: Strict schema compliance keeps the PRD consumable by downstream agents and tools.
acceptance_criteria: The UI validates the document on load and again before any canonical save or export.
Validation messages include the JSON path or section, the failing rule, and a human-readable message.
The UI blocks canonical writeback while required schema violations remain unresolved.

functional.title: Edit PRD by section
functional.description: The UI should let reviewers edit PRD content through section-specific forms while keeping a raw JSON editor available for maintainers.
functional.rationale: Section-specific editing lowers accidental schema breakage while raw JSON access remains useful for power users.
acceptance_criteria: The UI provides schema-driven section editors for MVP editing sections: requirements, user_stories, project_tracking, risks, open_questions, decisions, and delivery.operational_readiness.
The schema-driven editors expose required fields, enum choices, nullable fields, and nested acceptance criteria without requiring reviewers to edit raw JSON for routine MVP changes.
List-based MVP editing sections support adding, removing, duplicating, and reordering items where schema.strict.json allows arrays.
Raw JSON edit mode remains available for maintainers for every top-level section and validates the full PRD before applying changes.
Unsaved changes remain local to the session until the user saves or exports, and the pending writeback preview identifies changed sections and representative JSON paths.

functional.title: Render traceability views
functional.description: The UI should derive and display traceability relationships among PRD entities as both a matrix and an interactive graph.
functional.rationale: Reviewers and agents need to inspect how goals, requirements, stories, decisions, risks, dependencies, and open questions connect.
acceptance_criteria: The UI renders a matrix for linkable PRD entities using identifiers present in the document, including project_tracking linked_entity_ids when the section exists.
The UI renders a graph view that exposes inbound and outbound relationships for a selected node.
Broken or missing references are flagged in both matrix and graph views.
The UI can export an agent-ready trace summary that references stable IDs.

functional.title: Export and write back safely
functional.description: The UI should support exporting a review snapshot and writing approved changes back to the canonical PRD.json file.
functional.rationale: Review is incomplete unless approved edits can be persisted without corrupting the canonical document.
acceptance_criteria: The UI previews the pending diff or changed sections before writeback is confirmed.
The UI can write validated changes back to the same canonical PRD.json file.
The UI can export a read-only review snapshot without changing the canonical file.
A failed writeback leaves the last valid canonical file intact.

functional.title: Support derived index or database later
functional.description: The UI could generate an optional derived index or database for faster search, persistent review state, or cross-PRD querying without replacing PRD.json as the source of truth.
functional.rationale: A derived store can improve workflow speed later, but must remain optional until collaboration needs justify added persistence complexity.
acceptance_criteria: The derived index can be rebuilt from the canonical PRD.json at any time.
Disabling or deleting the derived index does not prevent viewing, validating, or writing back PRD.json.
The UI labels derived store data as cache, index, or workflow data rather than authoritative document content.
A future DB migration proposal identifies which data remains canonical JSON and which data becomes application workflow state.

functional.title: Show approval readiness
functional.description: The UI should show whether the loaded PRD is ready for approval based on schema validity, must-have coverage, open blockers, project-tracking status, and unresolved decisions.
functional.rationale: Approval owners need a compact readiness view before signing off on a PRD draft.
acceptance_criteria: The readiness view lists schema failures, active project-tracking blockers, unresolved open questions, proposed decisions, high-score risks, missing must-requirement story coverage, and broken traceability links that block approval.
The readiness view distinguishes blockers from warnings, where blockers prevent approval and warnings identify follow-up review work such as unassigned project-tracking owners or stale status notes.
Each readiness signal includes a category, severity, source section, and stable PRD ID or JSON path when available.
Approval guidance is derived from PRD content, schema validation results, schema compatibility results, and derived traceability only, not hidden application-only state.
The exported review snapshot includes the same readiness categories and severity labels shown in the UI.

functional.title: View agent context efficiency metrics
functional.description: A future UI may summarize content-redacted MCP metrics for focused reads, packet token budgets, truncation, and full-document fallbacks.
functional.rationale: Context efficiency should be measured at the selection layer agents actually use, without coupling the reviewer MVP to an optional encoding format.
acceptance_criteria: The UI reads content-redacted pacs-prd MCP metrics without reading PRD payloads from the metrics log.
The UI displays focused-read rate, packet token estimates, budget truncation, and full-document fallback counts.
The UI can filter metrics by MCP resource or tool without treating metrics data as canonical PRD content.

functional.title: Show schema version and extension compatibility
functional.description: The UI should read meta.schema_contract, schema.versions.json, and optional extensions metadata to show whether a loaded PRD is exactly compatible, backward compatible, legacy unversioned, migration-required, or blocked by unsupported required extensions.
functional.rationale: The PRD system is now a framework with multiple possible PRD implementations, so users need explicit compatibility status before reviewing, editing, or handing documents to agents.
acceptance_criteria: The UI displays the PRD document version separately from the schema contract version.
The UI reports exact, backward-compatible, migration-required, unknown, or legacy-unversioned status using schema.versions.json.
The UI lists declared extensions, required extensions, unsupported extensions, and extension schema references when present.

The UI blocks unsafe writeback when a required extension is unsupported, when a migration-required schema version has not been migrated, or when the viewer cannot preserve a loaded project_tracking section losslessly.

functional.title: Render first-class project tracking
functional.description: The UI should render a dedicated top-level project_tracking section with overall status, pending work, issues found, blockers, owners, linked entities, external workflow references, and status notes while preserving the section through load, edit, export, and writeback.
functional.rationale: Project execution state should live in the canonical PRD instead of being reconstructed from scattered readiness notes, questions, and decisions.
acceptance_criteria: The UI exposes project_tracking as a first-class section in navigation, section detail, dashboard summaries, change preview, and review snapshot export.
The UI shows overall status, owner, pending work, issues found, blockers, and notes even when some linked_entity_ids are broken, and it surfaces those broken links instead of dropping the records.
The structured project-tracking editor preserves and validates optional external_refs URLs for PTW, PTI, PTB, and PTN records.
The UI validates the full PRD before applying project_tracking edits and preserves project_tracking losslessly through export and canonical writeback.

functional.title: Provide a reusable local framework and MCP-ready foundation
functional.description: The viewer should refine the PRD framework through its own implementation and define a future local MCP server path, using the official Model Context Protocol TypeScript SDK, so a solo developer can reuse the final framework, viewer, and derived MCP access patterns to build later projects without treating the full PRD as the default agent payload.
functional.rationale: The viewer is both a product and a proving ground for the framework; making the reusable framework path explicit prevents the implementation from becoming a one-off tool that cannot scale to later solo-developer projects or agentic workflows.
acceptance_criteria: The PRD explicitly defines a future local MCP layer that exposes derived summaries, section packets, traceability packets, readiness, compatibility, and project-tracking views without replacing PRD.json as the canonical artifact.

The future MCP server direction is aligned to the official Model Context Protocol TypeScript SDK so the viewer stack, framework evolution, and solo-developer reuse path stay on one supported TypeScript implementation track.
The reusable-framework path keeps local-first operation, modest hardware expectations, and low-overhead derived access as baseline constraints for later solo-developer projects.

The future MCP path includes local metrics for request volume, response size, latency, cache behavior, and full-document fallback so the solo developer can later verify whether the server meaningfully reduces agent context cost.

non_functional.description: The UI should load and render a PRD.json file quickly enough for interactive review on a standard developer laptop.
non_functional.target: Initial dashboard render completes in 2 seconds or less for a PRD.json file up to 1 MB with up to 1,000 linked entities.
acceptance_criteria: The initial dashboard appears within the target time on the benchmark fixture.
Section-to-section navigation remains responsive within 300 ms for typical review actions.
Traceability generation completes within 3 seconds on the same fixture.

non_functional.description: The UI should restrict writeback and admin actions to explicit authorized local sessions for the MVP, with stronger identity-based authorization deferred until any shared deployment is approved.
non_functional.target: No browser session can modify PRD.json unless the user explicitly grants a writable local file handle for that session; shared deployments must add identity-based authorization before enabling writeback.
acceptance_criteria: Read-only file-picker sessions cannot trigger a same-file save or writeback request.
Writable sessions require an explicit File System Access API handle or equivalent local-shell permission before the Save action is enabled.
Unsupported browsers and sessions without a writable handle remain export-only and show a clear writeback limitation.
Any future shared or hosted deployment disables writeback until user identity, authorization policy, and audit expectations are defined.

non_functional.description: The UI should keep PRD content local by default and avoid external transmission unless the user explicitly opts in.
non_functional.target: No PRD content is sent to third-party services during open, edit, validate, or save flows by default.
acceptance_criteria: The default open, edit, validate, and save path makes no outbound third-party network calls containing PRD content.
Exporting or sharing content requires an explicit user action.
Any optional external integration is disabled until configured by the operator.

non_functional.description: The UI must preserve the canonical PRD.json file when save or export operations fail.
non_functional.target: Interrupted or failed writeback leaves the last valid PRD.json readable and unchanged.
acceptance_criteria: A failed save does not replace the last valid canonical file with partial content.
Validation failure prevents a corrupt writeback from starting.
The UI can recover from an interrupted staged write without losing the previous document version.

non_functional.description: The core dashboard, editors, validation panels, and traceability views should be operable with accessible controls.
non_functional.target: Core UI paths meet WCAG 2.1 AA expectations.
acceptance_criteria: Core actions are reachable by keyboard without a mouse.
Focus states and form labels are visible and programmatically associated.
Validation errors are announced to assistive technology.
Keyboard and screen-reader users can review project-tracking status badges, blocker lists, item rows, and linked-entity navigation with visible focus states and labels.

non_functional.description: The UI should emit structured events for key review and writeback actions.
non_functional.target: Load, validate, save, export, and traceability operations produce timestamped, document-scoped logs.
acceptance_criteria: Each core action writes a structured event with an action name, timestamp, and outcome.
Error logs include enough context to diagnose the failure without exposing full PRD content by default.
Reviewers can inspect the most recent local or application logs during troubleshooting.

non_functional.description: The viewer should preserve project_tracking losslessly across load, edit, export, and save for every supported schema fixture.
non_functional.target: 100% project-tracking round-trip fidelity for supported schema fixtures.
acceptance_criteria: Loading and saving a PRD with project_tracking preserves all PTW, PTI, PTB, and PTN records without dropping linked_entity_ids, linked_prd_ids, or external_refs.
Validation failures in project_tracking block writeback without replacing the last valid loaded PRD.
Regression fixtures cover empty, populated, and malformed project_tracking sections.

statement.i_want: to review PRD.json in a structured web view with dashboard summaries and section navigation
statement.so_that: I can confirm the proposed scope and blockers without reading raw JSON

acceptance_criteria.given: a valid PRD.json file is loaded
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI renders section summaries for goals, requirements, user stories, risks, decisions, and open questions

acceptance_criteria.given: a dashboard item shows a blocker
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI navigates to the relevant section and item

acceptance_criteria.given: the PRD has validation errors
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI renders section summaries for goals, requirements, user stories, project tracking, risks, decisions, and open questions

edge_cases: The PRD contains malformed JSON and only an error state can be rendered.
A linked item referenced from the review view does not exist in the current revision.
The document is large enough that navigation must remain responsive across sections.

statement.i_want: to validate PRD.json against the strict schema and ID rules
statement.so_that: I can catch structural and traceability errors before the document is shared for approval or implementation

acceptance_criteria.given: a PRD file is loaded into the validator
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI lists schema errors with JSON paths and human-readable messages

acceptance_criteria.given: two items share the same ID or a link points to a missing ID
acceptance_criteria.then: the summary flags each broken reference

acceptance_criteria.given: the document contains unresolved validation failures
acceptance_criteria.when: I attempt to write back to PRD.json
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI blocks the write and leaves the canonical file unchanged

acceptance_criteria.given: a loaded PRD contains malformed project_tracking data
acceptance_criteria.when: I validate or attempt to save the document
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI reports the failing project_tracking path and blocks canonical writeback

edge_cases: A required top-level section is missing from the document.
A date-time field uses an invalid format.
A required array is empty even though the schema expects at least one item.
project_tracking entries reference missing entities.

statement.i_want: to trace each requirement to its stories, decisions, dependencies, and source context
statement.so_that: I can implement the right scope and report missing context before acting

acceptance_criteria.given: a requirement is selected
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI shows linked stories, decisions, dependencies, and unresolved questions where available

acceptance_criteria.given: I request an agent-ready summary
acceptance_criteria.when: the UI generates the trace bundle
acceptance_criteria.then: the output includes stable IDs and current statuses for each linked item

acceptance_criteria.given: a linked item changes in a new revision
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI highlights affected relationships

edge_cases: A requirement has multiple linked stories and the trace view must show all of them.
A requirement is removed but still referenced by an older story.
An open question exists without a linked requirement or story.

statement.i_want: to review unresolved blockers and approval readiness from the reviewer UI
statement.so_that: only internally consistent PRDs are signed off

acceptance_criteria.given: the PRD has open questions, schema failures, or unresolved must-have coverage
acceptance_criteria.when: I open the approval readiness view
acceptance_criteria.then: the blockers are shown before approval is recommended

acceptance_criteria.given: the PRD has no blocking issues
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI shows a ready state that references the validation timestamp and source PRD file

acceptance_criteria.given: validation is stale because the document changed
acceptance_criteria.when: I open the approval readiness view
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI requires validation to rerun before approval readiness is considered current

acceptance_criteria.given: the loaded PRD contains an active project_tracking blocker
acceptance_criteria.then: the blocker appears alongside schema, question, decision, and traceability blockers before approval is recommended

edge_cases: Multiple approvers need separate signoff outside the MVP UI.
Approval is attempted while a high-score risk remains unresolved.
Readiness output is exported but the canonical file changes afterward.

statement.i_want: to view focused-read and packet-budget efficiency from content-redacted MCP metrics
statement.so_that: I can tune agent context budgets and detect unnecessary full-document fallbacks

acceptance_criteria.given: the MCP metrics log contains focused requests and full-document fallbacks
acceptance_criteria.when: I open the context efficiency dashboard
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI shows request counts, focused-read rate, fallback rate, and failure categories

acceptance_criteria.given: packet metrics include token estimates and budget metadata
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI shows preset, estimated tokens, configured budget, and whether content was truncated

acceptance_criteria.given: an agent requests a full-document payload
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI records the fallback separately from focused reads without storing PRD content

edge_cases: The metrics log does not exist yet because no MCP request has run.
A JSONL row is malformed or from an older event version.
A packet is truncated because selected content exceeds its token budget.

statement.i_want: to see schema version, migration, and extension compatibility before editing a PRD
statement.so_that: I can avoid corrupting documents that use a newer framework version or unsupported required extensions

acceptance_criteria.given: a loaded PRD declares meta.schema_contract.schema_version
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI shows declared schema version, current schema version, compatibility mode, and migration status

acceptance_criteria.given: a loaded PRD has no schema_contract declaration
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI marks it as legacy unversioned while still allowing schema validation against the selected strict schema

acceptance_criteria.given: a loaded PRD declares a required extension that the viewer does not support
acceptance_criteria.then: the UI blocks writeback or requires explicit authorized override with preservation of extension data

edge_cases: The schema manifest is missing or cannot be parsed.
The PRD declares a future schema version unknown to the current viewer.
An optional extension has data but no registered schema reference.
A future schema version requires project_tracking support.

statement.i_want: to record project status, pending work, issues, blockers, and notes in a first-class project_tracking section
statement.so_that: the execution state stays attached to the canonical PRD instead of being split across readiness notes, questions, and separate issue lists

acceptance_criteria.given: the viewer loads a PRD that contains project_tracking
acceptance_criteria.then: I can review overall status, pending work, issues found, blockers, owners, and notes in one structured view

acceptance_criteria.given: a project-tracking item links to requirements, stories, risks, questions, decisions, or other tracking items
acceptance_criteria.then: the linked_entity_ids are visible and broken links are called out instead of silently omitted

acceptance_criteria.given: I update project-tracking content through the viewer
acceptance_criteria.when: I save or export the PRD
acceptance_criteria.then: the section remains schema-valid and round-trips without losing status metadata or links

edge_cases: The PRD uses project_tracking but the owner fields are still null.
A blocker links to a decision that has since been superseded.
A maintainer removes a project-tracking row that is still referenced by another tracking item.

statement.i_want: the viewer and PRD framework to expose a future local MCP layer using the official TypeScript SDK
statement.so_that: I can reuse the final framework, viewer, and derived PRD access patterns to build later projects without paying full-document token costs on every agent task

acceptance_criteria.given: I am working on a later project that uses the same PRD framework
acceptance_criteria.when: I need agent access to the project PRD
acceptance_criteria.then: the framework defines a local MCP-ready path for section, entity, traceability, readiness, and project-tracking projections instead of requiring full-document reads by default

acceptance_criteria.given: the future MCP layer is introduced
acceptance_criteria.then: it uses the official TypeScript MCP SDK and keeps PRD.json as the canonical artifact while any index or cache remains derived

acceptance_criteria.given: I run the stack on a solo-developer machine
acceptance_criteria.when: the viewer and future MCP layer are both active
acceptance_criteria.then: the design remains local-first and avoids unnecessary background infrastructure or heavyweight hosted dependencies

edge_cases: The MCP layer falls back to full-document reads too often and does not materially reduce agent payload size.
A derived local index becomes stale and disagrees with the canonical PRD.json.
The future SDK choice drifts away from the viewer's TypeScript implementation and fragments maintenance.

technical.description: PRD.json must remain the canonical artifact in v1; any database, cache, or search index must be derived from that file and must not become the source of truth.
technical.impact: Keeps the reviewer workflow simple, reviewable in Git, and aligned with AI-agent consumption.

technical.description: Every load, edit, save, and export must be validated against schema.strict.json and must fail closed on violations rather than silently repairing data.
technical.impact: Prevents schema drift and ensures deterministic reviewer feedback.

technical.description: The MVP must run local-first in a browser or thin local shell and support explicit file open/save without requiring a mandatory backend service.
technical.impact: Reduces setup friction and keeps the initial delivery viable with the current JSON-first repo.

technical.description: Serialization must be deterministic and semantically lossless so reviewers get stable diffs and no accidental document drift on round-trip.
technical.impact: Preserves review trust and avoids noisy rewrites that obscure meaningful changes.

technical.description: Any collaboration layer added later must be additive and non-authoritative; it cannot replace PRD.json as the canonical artifact without a separate migration PRD.
technical.impact: Allows future comments, assignments, or sync features without forcing premature persistence architecture.

technical.description: Release gating must include schema validation, round-trip fixtures, accessibility checks, and large-document smoke tests before promotion.
technical.impact: Blocks regressions that would make the reviewer UI unsafe to trust.

legal_compliance.requirement: If reviewer identities, comments, access logs, or exported artifacts are persisted or shared, minimize collected personal data, define retention and deletion rules, and protect access appropriately before any shared deployment.
legal_compliance.evidence_source: Project context: Mexico-based workspace and potential reviewer identity data

dependencies.name: schema.strict.json contract
dependencies.description: The reviewer UI depends on a stable strict schema contract for validation, save gating, and regression tests.

dependencies.name: Representative PRD.json fixture corpus
dependencies.description: The UI needs representative valid PRDs and malformed edge cases to test rendering, validation, traceability, and round-trip behavior.

dependencies.name: Local file access runtime
dependencies.description: The MVP depends on browser file open/save APIs or a thin local shell that can access PRD.json directly.

dependencies.name: Git history and PR workflow
dependencies.description: Reviewer changes should be persisted as file diffs and reviewable changes in source control rather than hidden application state.

dependencies.name: Deferred shared collaboration store
dependencies.description: Only needed if concurrent editing, threaded comments, assignments, or cross-session history become required; until then, derive state from JSON and Git history.

dependencies.name: PACS MCP metrics log
dependencies.description: The PACS context workflow records content-redacted focused-read, packet-size, and full-document-fallback metrics at .metrics/pacs_prd_mcp.jsonl.

dependencies.name: Schema version manifest
dependencies.description: The reviewer UI and compatibility tooling depend on schema.versions.json to resolve current schema version, compatibility policy, migration requirements, and extension behavior.

dependencies.name: Official MCP TypeScript SDK
dependencies.description: The reusable framework path depends on the official TypeScript MCP SDK as the supported base for a future local MCP server that serves derived PRD packets without replacing the canonical JSON artifact.

milestones.name: Scope and contract freeze
milestones.deliverable: Approved reviewer workflow map, canonical JSON editing rules, and schema validation behavior.
entry_criteria: DB decision captured as deferred and derived for v1
exit_criteria: Scope, file boundaries, and validation gate signed off
MVP and future collaboration boundaries documented

milestones.name: Read-only reviewer MVP
milestones.deliverable: UI can load PRD.json, render dashboard and sections, and show validation results.
exit_criteria: Load, search, and validation work on sample files
Traceability data can be derived for at least requirements and stories

milestones.name: Edit and save path
milestones.deliverable: Editable document UI with deterministic save/export and round-trip checks.

milestones.name: Pilot review workflow
milestones.deliverable: Small-group review flow with readiness summary, traceability, and acceptance handoff.
exit_criteria: Pilot reviewers complete at least one full review cycle
DB/collaboration requests are logged for later decision

milestones.name: Launch readiness and handoff
milestones.deliverable: Release candidate, support runbook, known limitations, and monitoring checklist.
exit_criteria: Fallback path to direct JSON editing documented

rollout_plan.strategy: Ship a local-first browser reviewer that reads and writes PRD.json directly, then expand from internal dogfood to a limited pilot before deciding whether any shared collaboration layer is justified. Keep any database path deferred and derived until collaboration use cases prove they need shared state.

phases.name: Dogfood
success_criteria: Sample files open and save locally
No data loss occurs on round-trip

phases.name: Limited pilot
success_criteria: At least three reviewers complete a full review

phases.name: Internal rollout
phases.audience: All PRD authors, reviewers, and implementation agents for this repo

phases.name: Collaboration expansion decision
success_criteria: Shared DB is approved only if collaboration demand is proven
Compliance owner signs off before shared identity or comments are persisted
Migration path remains derived from JSON unless a separate PRD supersedes this decision

communications: Announce that PRD.json is the canonical artifact in v1 and invalid saves are blocked.
Publish a short setup guide for opening, validating, editing, and exporting PRD.json.
Share a pilot schedule, feedback channel, and known limitations before rollout.
Explain that collaboration features and any DB layer will be introduced only if the pilot proves the need.
training: Run a 15-minute walkthrough of file open, dashboard, validation, traceability, edit, and save/export flows.
Provide a one-page checklist for schema errors, diff review, and recovery steps.
Give reviewers a checklist for approval readiness and decision capture.
support_handoff: Name the first-line support owner and escalation path for schema or save failures.
Create an issue template for validation, serialization, rendering, and traceability defects.
Document the fallback path to direct JSON editing if the UI is unavailable.
Publish release notes and known limitations for pilot users.

operational_readiness.notes: Shared fixture factories now cover valid PRDs, malformed JSON, schema-invalid PRDs, unsupported schema versions, required extensions, and broken trace links; validation, compatibility, traceability, load, validate, edit/apply, export, snapshot export, and blocked writeback regression tests verify fail-closed behavior without replacing the last valid loaded PRD.

Status tiles and readiness blocker signals now act as one-action navigation controls; section-backed targets update the selected section visibly, and destination panels receive focus for keyboard and screen-reader continuity.

The section editor now provides structured MVP editors for requirements, user stories, risks, open questions, decisions, and delivery.operational_readiness, including schema-backed required fields, enum controls, nullable fields, nested acceptance criteria, and add/remove/duplicate/reorder controls; raw JSON maintainer mode remains available and both edit paths validate the full PRD before applying changes.

The readiness panel now derives categorized blocker and warning signals from schema validation, open questions, decisions, high-score risks, must-have story coverage, traceability, and schema compatibility; each signal exposes severity, source section, stable ID or JSON path, and the exported review snapshot carries the same readiness categories.

The viewer now has a representative large-PRD fixture with schema coverage, a browser smoke test for large-document load/render/navigation thresholds, and an accessibility pass covering keyboard dashboard navigation, focus transitions, active section semantics, readiness signal labels, live validation messages, and traceability graph keyboard alternatives.

Browser file open, section-scoped JSON edits, pending writeback preview by changed section and sampled JSON paths, strict validation before apply/export/save, read-only PRD and review snapshot exports, and File System Access API writeback are implemented; failed saves keep pending changes local and unsupported browsers remain export-only.
Only needed if the app moves beyond local-only usage.
No named compliance owner has been assigned yet; finalize retention and identity handling before any shared deployment.

The viewer now records local-only structured diagnostic events for load, validate, edit, export, save, and failure paths; events are shown in a diagnostics panel, can be exported as a local diagnostic snapshot, and are sanitized to exclude PRD content, secrets, raw JSON, and large field values.

A local MVP runbook in viewer/docs/local-mvp-runbook.md now covers setup, validation gates, browser limitations, failed writeback recovery, malformed PRD reporting, and triage ownership for schema issues, UI bugs, PRD content problems, and compliance concerns.

The local MCP server provides focused discovery and token-budgeted agent packets, records content-redacted request and fallback metrics, and leaves any future viewer visualization deferred outside the MVP.
Initial Vite, React, and TypeScript scaffold lives under viewer/ with source files, tool-stack notes, and read-only PRD summary wiring.
Vitest plus Testing Library provide green unit and component coverage, while Playwright Test provides green Chromium E2E coverage for the current read-only reviewer workflow.
The viewer reads schema.versions.json plus meta.schema_contract to report exact, legacy, unknown, migration-required, and required-extension compatibility states before unsafe writeback.
The viewer validates the loaded PRD against schema.strict.json in-browser on load and from the Validate action, with status and issue counts visible in the dashboard.

The viewer can open a local JSON file through the browser File API, validate it, replace the active dashboard only when schema-valid, and show parse/schema errors without corrupting the current document view.
The selected-section workspace now derives reviewable item rows with IDs, status metadata, titles, and summaries so reviewers can inspect PRD content without reading raw JSON.

The viewer derives requirement, story, decision, dependency, and persona relationships from stable PRD IDs, renders a traceability matrix, and flags broken references that schema pattern validation cannot catch.

operational_readiness.area: Traceability graph and agent trace export
operational_readiness.notes: The viewer renders a selected-node React Flow graph for inbound and outbound trace links, keeps broken references visible in focused graph state, and exports an agent-ready trace summary JSON bundle with stable IDs and current statuses.

DEC-011 selects a separate clean-history public repository so unrelated private history is never copied. The current tree and validation gates are ready; the clean snapshot, public remote, and final visibility verification remain.

project_tracking.summary: The local-first reviewer MVP and current-tree public repository preparation are complete, including the linked Viewer TRD, public contribution and security guidance, automated validation gates, clean dependency audits, and fast-forward merge readiness. DEC-011 requires publication from a separate clean-history repository so unrelated private history remains private; shared-deployment governance remains a separate blocker.

pending_work.title: Implement viewer project-tracking surface
pending_work.description: Surface project_tracking in the dashboard, section rail, section detail, change preview, review snapshot, and validated writeback so reviewers can track pending work, issues, blockers, and notes inside the canonical PRD.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in the viewer section model, dashboard metrics, dedicated project-tracking panel, selected-section rendering, approval-readiness signals, change preview diffing, and review snapshot export. project_tracking is now visible as a first-class top-level section and preserves through validated export and writeback flows because the viewer edits and serializes the full canonical PRD document.

pending_work.title: Add structured editing and validation for project tracking
pending_work.description: Extend schema-driven editing, load/save/export paths, and regression coverage to preserve project_tracking losslessly and block invalid edits.
pending_work.notes: Implemented with schema-driven project_tracking editing for overview fields, pending work, issues, blockers, and notes; the viewer now validates the full PRD before applying structured edits, preserves project_tracking through export and writeback flows, and blocks invalid project_tracking changes without replacing the active valid document. Regression coverage now exercises valid apply/export behavior and fail-closed invalid edit handling.

pending_work.title: Resolve remaining governance blockers for release readiness
pending_work.description: Assign ownership for compliance and launch decisions, then close or accept the remaining proposed decisions and open questions that still block readiness.
pending_work.notes: The local-only MVP is functionally ahead of the PRD governance state.

pending_work.title: Freeze the local MCP contract and implementation plan
pending_work.description: Define the local-only MCP server contract, resource and tool surface, read-only safety rules, source-file selection rules, response-shaping modes, and phased delivery plan for the official TypeScript SDK implementation.
pending_work.notes: Frozen in mcp/README.md plus mcp/src/surface.ts: stdio-first, local-only, read-only, viewer/PRD_web_ui.json as the default artifact, planned resource and tool inventory, and planned response-shaping modes.

pending_work.title: Scaffold the local TypeScript MCP server
pending_work.description: Create the MCP server package, official TypeScript SDK wiring, local scripts, and a minimal health or info capability so the server can run and be inspected locally.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in mcp/ with the official TypeScript SDK, local npm scripts, a stdio entrypoint, and the initial prd://server/info resource plus server_info tool.

pending_work.title: Implement canonical PRD loading, validation, and reload detection
pending_work.description: Load the active PRD JSON from disk, validate it against schema.strict.json, cache the parsed artifact, and reload only when file contents or metadata change.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in mcp/src/prd-loader.ts with stat-based cache reuse, SHA-256 file snapshots, strict schema validation via Ajv, schema compatibility checks from schema.versions.json, fail-closed blocking for malformed JSON, schema-invalid PRDs, required extensions, and lossy compatibility states, plus focused loader regression tests.

pending_work.title: Build the normalized in-memory PRD index
pending_work.description: Derive stable indexes by section, entity ID, and trace link so later MCP resources and tools can serve compact targeted views instead of re-scanning the whole PRD on every request.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in mcp/src/prd-index.ts with cached section, entity, inbound-link, outbound-link, and broken-link indexes built only from schema-valid PRDs; server_info now exposes index snapshots and regression tests cover valid builds, broken references, cache reuse, and blocked upstream loads.

pending_work.title: Expose core MCP resources for compact PRD reads
pending_work.description: Add resources for summary, named sections, entity lookups, trace views, readiness, compatibility, and project_tracking so agents can request focused PRD context instead of the full document.
pending_work.notes: Implemented via static resources for summary, compatibility, readiness, and project tracking plus templated resources for section, entity, and trace reads; server_info still exposes server/load/index diagnostics and focused resource builders are covered by resource regression tests.

pending_work.title: Expose core MCP tools for search and agent packets
pending_work.description: Add search, entity, linked-entity, blocker, open-question, proposed-decision, and agent-packet tools so solo-developer agent workflows can ask for only the minimum needed PRD context.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in mcp/src/tools.ts plus server registrations for search_prd, get_entity, get_linked_entities, list_blockers, list_open_questions, list_proposed_decisions, and build_agent_packet; outputs stay compact by default and regression tests cover search ranking, linked-entity lookup, unresolved lists, and compact packet assembly.

pending_work.title: Add packet compaction modes and token-cost estimates
pending_work.description: Support compact, standard, and full response modes for MCP tools and resources, and include byte and token estimates so the solo developer can compare focused packets against full-document fallback reads.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in mcp/src/response-modes.ts and wired through mcp/src/server.ts so resources accept ?mode=compact|standard|full and tools accept a mode input; every wrapped response now returns byte, character, and approximate token estimates plus savings against the active canonical PRD size, and regression tests in mcp/src/response-modes.test.ts cover shaping and estimate behavior.

pending_work.title: Add local MCP metrics and diagnostics
pending_work.description: Record local-only structured metrics for request type, latency, response size, cache hits, source artifact, and full-document fallback rate so the team can later judge whether the MCP layer is useful without logging PRD content.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in mcp/src/metrics.ts and wired through mcp/src/server.ts so every resource and tool request appends a local JSONL metric record with request channel, target, requested mode, sanitized input metadata, load and index cache state, latency, response byte and token estimates, and focused-vs-full-document scope; the metrics writer creates the parent directory on demand, avoids payload logging, redacts free-text inputs, and regression tests in mcp/src/metrics.test.ts cover redaction and failure-path recording.

pending_work.title: Add safety gates, fixtures, and MCP integration coverage
pending_work.description: Cover malformed PRDs, unsupported schema versions, required extensions, broken trace links, invalid resource or tool requests, and inspector-level integration flows so the local MCP server fails closed and stays trustworthy.
pending_work.notes: Implemented with reusable local fixtures in mcp/src/test-fixtures.ts, loader and index fixture coverage updates, stdio integration coverage in mcp/src/server.integration.test.ts, and query-capable resource templates in mcp/src/server.ts so summary, readiness, compatibility, project-tracking, server-info, section, entity, and trace reads can be exercised through the real MCP transport; the package README now documents the local MCP Inspector workflow and the regression suite covers blocked loads, broken trace links, invalid resource requests, invalid tool calls, and failure-path metric emission.

pending_work.title: Add optional localhost HTTP transport after stdio stabilizes
pending_work.description: Once the stdio-first server is stable, add optional localhost-only HTTP transport and origin checks for clients that benefit from browser-friendly or remote-process attachment without changing the local-only trust model.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in mcp/src/http-server.ts and mcp/src/index.ts with optional Streamable HTTP transport selected by PACS_MCP_TRANSPORT=http, localhost-only host validation, optional explicit origin allowlists, health checks, and matching npm scripts; mcp/src/http-server.test.ts covers localhost client success and non-local origin rejection, and the package README documents the runtime contract and environment variables.

pending_work.title: Implement the reusable PACS project bootstrap layer
pending_work.description: Add a project scaffold, initialization script, validation coverage, and local MCP registration guidance so new projects can adopt the PACS framework without manual file copying.
pending_work.notes: Implemented with template-backed scaffolding in templates/pacs-project/, the metrics-enabled tools/init_pacs_project.py bootstrap script, regression coverage in tools/test_init_pacs_project.py, a root npm init:project command, and updated MCP/extraction docs that show how a new project registers the shared pacs-prd server over stdio or optional localhost HTTP.

pending_work.title: Link project tracking to external workflow records
pending_work.description: Add optional external_refs URLs to project-tracking records, expose them in structured editing, and document when PRD, TRD, GitHub issues, tasks, and sprints are updated.
pending_work.notes: Implemented in schema 1.2.0, the viewer project-tracking editor, PACS bootstrap output, and docs/delivery-workflow.md. External systems remain authoritative for granular execution state.

pending_work.title: Prepare the Viewer branch and repository for public release
pending_work.description: Document the Viewer technical design, add public contribution and security boundaries, automate the release validation gates, remove generated test artifacts from version control, verify focused MCP configuration, and produce merge-readiness evidence for PRD-v1.
pending_work.notes: Current-tree work is complete with viewer/TRD.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, SECURITY.md, aligned source-of-truth guidance, a GitHub Actions validation workflow, clean npm audits, removal of tracked Playwright output, schema and agent validation, 32 MCP tests, 63 Viewer unit/component tests, 10 Viewer browser tests, plugin rebuild, secret-pattern scans, and verified fast-forward ancestry to PRD-v1. DEC-011 now requires a separate clean-history public repository; add its canonical URL after creation and complete final public visibility verification.

issues_found.title: No first-class project status surface in the viewer
issues_found.description: Reviewers still have to infer TODOs, blockers, and known issues from readiness, operational_readiness, open questions, and decisions instead of reading one structured project state section.
issues_found.notes: Resolved by first-class project_tracking rendering in the viewer dashboard, navigation, section detail, readiness, and snapshot flows.

issues_found.title: Current viewer implementation hardcodes top-level section handling
issues_found.description: The viewer currently assumes the existing section set for summaries, navigation, change previews, and section detail rendering, so new core sections require explicit implementation work.
issues_found.notes: Without follow-up implementation, project_tracking would exist in the PRD but remain invisible in the UI.

issues_found.title: Project tracking can drift from risks, questions, and decisions
issues_found.description: If teams duplicate the same state across project_tracking, risks, open questions, decisions, and readiness notes without clear boundaries, reviewers may trust conflicting project status signals.
issues_found.notes: Resolved by docs/delivery-workflow.md, which defines source-of-truth boundaries and lifecycle transitions for PRD, TRD, GitHub issues, tasks, and sprints.

issues_found.title: Repeated full-document PRD reads remain a token sink for agent workflows
issues_found.description: Without a focused local MCP access layer, solo-developer agent workflows still tend to load more PRD context than they need, which wastes tokens and makes later framework reuse less efficient.
issues_found.notes: This is a quality and scale problem for the framework path, not a blocker for the current local-only viewer MVP.

blockers.title: Viewer implementation needed structured project_tracking editing
blockers.description: The viewer previously rendered project_tracking without structured editing or dedicated lossless regression coverage, which left the local editing path incomplete.
blockers.unblock_criteria: Implemented structured editing, validation-specific failure handling, and regression coverage for project_tracking.
blockers.notes: Cleared by the schema-driven project_tracking editor, full-document validation before apply, preserved export/writeback flows, and dedicated regression coverage for valid and invalid project_tracking edits.

blockers.title: Release governance still lacks named ownership and final decisions
blockers.description: The viewer PRD still has unresolved launch and compliance ownership plus several proposed decisions, so readiness remains blocked even though the local-only MVP is materially complete.
blockers.unblock_criteria: Assign owners, resolve the open questions, and accept or retire the remaining proposed decisions.
blockers.notes: Project tracking should make this mismatch visible without requiring manual synthesis across sections.

blockers.title: Public release must not inherit unrelated private history
blockers.description: The source repository contains unrelated private history that must never enter the public repository object database.
blockers.unblock_criteria: Publish only from a new repository initialized from a sanitized current-tree snapshot with no inherited Git objects.
blockers.notes: Cleared by DEC-011. The private source repository and its history remain unchanged; only the validated framework and Viewer snapshot may enter the public repository.

notes.note: project_tracking records execution state and follow-up work; risks stay future-facing, open_questions stay unresolved unknowns, decisions stay architectural or product choices, and delivery remains milestone and readiness planning.
The local-only MVP is functionally complete; project_tracking now has first-class rendering and structured editing support, and the remaining work is shared-deployment and governance readiness.

The viewer is intended to build and refine its own framework so that a solo developer can later reuse the final schema, review UI, and future local MCP layer to build other projects with lower agent context cost.

The first MCP milestone should stay local-only, read-only, stdio-first, and officially aligned to the TypeScript SDK; packet compaction and usefulness metrics should be in place before optional HTTP transport or any write-capable tooling is considered.

The reusable multi-project extraction path is now documented in docs/framework-extraction-plan.md with a concrete per-project config contract example in docs/pacs.config.example.json, so future PACS adopters can instantiate one PRD plus one MCP server instance per project instead of treating this repo layout as the only supported shape.

risks.description: Schema drift or validator mismatch causes the UI to accept or reject documents differently from schema.strict.json.
risks.mitigation: Lock schema versioning, add fixture regression tests, and fail closed on every write path.
risks.trigger: schema.strict.json changes without matching test updates or validation regressions appear in fixtures.

risks.description: Teams push for threaded comments, approvals, or concurrent editing before the local review flow is stable, pulling the project into premature collaboration architecture.
risks.mitigation: Keep v1 local-first and canonical-JSON-only, and gate any shared collaboration layer behind an explicit usage threshold.
risks.trigger: Requests for multi-user editing or shared comments appear before the pilot has validated the basic review flow.

risks.description: Browser file access or local shell packaging behaves differently across environments, breaking open/save or export flows.
risks.mitigation: Support standard file dialogs, provide explicit export paths, and keep a packaged local fallback option available.
risks.trigger: Users cannot reliably open or save PRD.json in the target browser or runtime.

risks.description: Round-trip serialization introduces noisy diffs or loses semantic content, undermining reviewer trust.
risks.mitigation: Use deterministic serialization, preserve IDs and field meaning, and compare saved output against fixture snapshots.
risks.trigger: Saved documents reopen with unrelated diffs, missing fields, or unstable field ordering.

risks.description: Privacy and compliance ambiguity blocks any shared or cloud-hosted deployment once reviewer identities, comments, or access logs are persisted.
risks.mitigation: Assign a compliance owner, define retention and access policies, and keep the first release local-only until policy is approved.
risks.trigger: A shared deployment or persisted review history is requested before compliance ownership is named.

risks.description: Large PRD files make validation and rendering slow enough to hurt adoption and make the UI feel unreliable.
risks.mitigation: Use incremental parsing, lazy rendering, and performance tests against the largest realistic PRD fixtures.
risks.trigger: p95 load or validation time crosses the target on representative large documents.

risks.description: A derived database or index becomes treated as authoritative by mistake, creating conflicts between UI state and PRD.json.
risks.mitigation: Label derived data clearly, rebuild indexes from PRD.json, and prohibit canonical writes from any store that cannot export a schema-valid PRD.json.
risks.trigger: Reviewers or agents reference data that exists only in a derived store and not in PRD.json.

risks.description: A PRD created for a different framework version or a required extension is edited as if it were core-only, causing data loss or invalid agent assumptions.
risks.mitigation: Use schema.versions.json, meta.schema_contract, and extension declarations to block unsafe writeback unless migration or extension support is confirmed.
risks.trigger: A PRD declares a future schema version, migration-required version, or unsupported required extension.

risks.description: Project-tracking entries become stale or duplicate external workflow state, causing reviewers to trust outdated status inside the PRD.
risks.mitigation: Keep project_tracking scoped to PRD execution status, warn on stale owners or notes, and document boundaries between project tracking, risks, open questions, decisions, and delivery readiness.
risks.trigger: Project-tracking status conflicts with delivery readiness, risks, or open questions without an updated note explaining the difference.

open_questions.question: What is the target launch date and pilot cohort for the first reviewer release?
Who owns compliance and privacy review for reviewer identities, comments, retention, and any future shared deployment?
At what collaboration threshold do we introduce a shared database or sync layer instead of relying on derived caches and Git history?
Do we need persistent inline comments or approvals in v1, or is read, edit, save, readiness export, and Git diff review enough?
Should the first implementation use a browser-only file API, a Vite local app with a thin file server, or a packaged local shell?
Which visual graph library should be selected for the traceability view after prototype validation?
Should optional encoding benchmark metrics be retained or exposed in the reviewer UI?
open_questions.resolution: No. Optional encoding benchmarks remain local and outside the reviewer and agent compliance paths; context efficiency is measured through content-redacted MCP metrics.

open_questions.question: Which extension namespaces should be supported first for users who adapt this PRD framework to different PRD methodologies or domain-specific requirement models?
Should public release rewrite the private source history or publish from a separate clean-history repository?
open_questions.resolution: Publish from a separate clean-history repository containing only the validated PRD framework and Viewer snapshot. Keep the source repository and all unrelated history private.

decisions.title: Canonical JSON first
decisions.statement: PRD.json remains the canonical source of truth in v1; any database, index, or shared store is deferred and derived unless collaboration needs justify it.
decisions.rationale: This keeps the reviewer MVP simple, preserves a single reviewable artifact for humans and AI agents, and avoids persistence migration work before the workflow proves value.

decisions.title: Strict schema gate
decisions.statement: schema.strict.json is the authoritative validation contract for every load, edit, save, and export.
decisions.rationale: A strict gate prevents silent drift and keeps reviewer feedback deterministic for humans and agents.

decisions.title: Local-first browser delivery
decisions.statement: The initial release is a local-first browser UI or thin local shell that can open, validate, and save PRD.json directly; a backend is optional, not required.
decisions.rationale: Direct file handling minimizes setup friction and fits the current JSON-first repo.

decisions.title: Git diffs as v1 audit trail
decisions.statement: Reviewer history in v1 is represented by Git diffs and file versions, not by a separate application-side revision store.
decisions.rationale: This keeps review artifacts aligned with the source file and avoids duplicate state.

decisions.title: Schema-driven UI preferred
decisions.statement: The implementation should prefer schema-driven section forms and validation over manually duplicating the PRD contract in UI code.
decisions.rationale: The repo already has a strict schema; reusing it reduces drift and makes future schema changes easier to support.

decisions.title: Focused MCP context is the default agent path
decisions.statement: Agents should use focused MCP discovery and token-budgeted build_agent_packet requests by default. Lightweight plugin hooks may inject routing guidance, while TOON remains an opt-in benchmark outside compliance, merge, release, and viewer paths.
decisions.rationale: Selection removes far more context than transcoding, preserves canonical JSON, works through an agent-facing tool contract, and avoids format-specific accuracy and maintenance costs.

decisions.title: Vite React TypeScript scaffold
decisions.statement: The PRD viewer starts as a Vite, React, and TypeScript app under viewer/, with Ajv for schema validation, JSON Forms for section editors, vanilla-jsoneditor for raw JSON mode, and @xyflow/react reserved for traceability graph work.
decisions.rationale: This stack matches the local-first browser constraint, supports schema-driven UI, keeps the initial implementation backend-free, and aligns the future editor and graph requirements without introducing an authoritative database.

decisions.title: TDD and Playwright test stack
decisions.statement: The viewer implementation uses Vitest with React Testing Library for fast unit and component tests, and Playwright Test as the required browser E2E framework.
decisions.rationale: Vitest aligns with the Vite app shell for quick local feedback, Testing Library keeps component tests focused on user-visible behavior, and Playwright covers real browser workflows required for file, validation, navigation, and export paths.

decisions.title: Approval readiness stays separate from project tracking
decisions.statement: The viewer should show approval readiness and project_tracking as related but distinct views: readiness answers whether the PRD can be approved, while project_tracking records execution status, pending work, issues, blockers, and notes.
decisions.rationale: Collapsing both concepts into one panel would mix approval gates with delivery state and make it harder to distinguish approval blockers from follow-up work.

decisions.title: Future local MCP layer uses the official TypeScript SDK
decisions.statement: When the viewer adds a local MCP server, it should use the official Model Context Protocol TypeScript SDK and serve derived PRD projections rather than making full-document JSON reads the default path for agentic workflows.
decisions.rationale: The viewer already lives in a TypeScript stack, and the reusable-framework goal is better served by one supported implementation path that stays local-first, canonical-JSON-first, and lightweight enough for solo-developer hardware.

decisions.title: Public release uses a separate clean-history repository
decisions.statement: Publish the PRD framework and Viewer from a new repository initialized from a sanitized current-tree snapshot; do not copy or rewrite the private source repository history.
decisions.rationale: A new repository creates a verifiable object-level boundary, keeps unrelated private history private, avoids coordinated force-push risk, and gives the public project a focused identity.
